try-except-basics.label: Try/Except Basics
try-except-basics.summary: Catch expected errors and keep programs usable.

sketch1.title: Only the risky line needs protection

sketch2.title: Use exceptions to keep programs usable

project.title: Practice safe integer conversion with try/except

q1.title: What happens after a caught conversion error?
q1.prompt:
A program runs this pattern:

```python
try:
    number = int(text)
except ValueError:
    print("Bad number")
```

If `text` is `"cat"`, what happens?
q1.hint: Think about what `int()` does with text that is not a valid whole number.

help.concept: `except ValueError` handles failures from `int()` when text cannot be converted to an integer.
help.hint_1: `int("cat")` cannot produce a number, so Python raises a `ValueError`.
help.hint_2: Because that specific error is caught, the program runs the `except` block instead of stopping immediately.

options.a: The `except` block runs and prints `Bad number`
options.b: The `try` block prints the converted number
options.c: Python ignores the bad text and uses 0
options.d: The program always stops before reaching `except`

q2.title: Which error fits int conversion?
q2.prompt: Which exception type should you catch when `int(user_text)` might fail because the text is not a valid integer?
q2.hint: Read the code or question carefully and choose the option that creates the exact behavior the prompt asks for.

help.concept: This question checks whether you can connect the prompt to the Python concept from the lesson.
help.hint_1: Focus on what the blank or answer stands for: a value, a method, a key, an index, or a variable name.
help.hint_2: Try each option in the sentence or code line and keep the one that makes the example work exactly as described.

q3.title: Why use try/except here?
q3.prompt: Choose all true reasons to use `try/except` around `int(text)` when cleaning messy input.
q3.hint: Look for benefits related to handling bad numeric text safely.

help.concept: `try/except` is useful when some values may be invalid and you want the program to respond safely instead of crashing.
help.hint_1: Think about what happens when one item is bad in a larger set of inputs.
help.hint_2: Correct choices should mention graceful handling and continued program flow, not guaranteed conversion success.

options.a: It lets the program handle invalid numeric text without crashing immediately
options.b: It can show a fallback message when conversion fails
options.c: It guarantees every string will become an integer
options.d: It can help a loop continue processing later values after one bad value

q4.title: Which inputs would trigger ValueError?
q4.prompt: Choose all values that would raise `ValueError` in `int(value)`.
q4.hint: Pick the strings that are not valid whole-number text.

help.concept: `int()` accepts strings like `"12"` or `"-3"`, but rejects text with letters or decimal points in string form for plain integer conversion.
help.hint_1: A minus sign with digits is still a valid integer string.
help.hint_2: Look for values containing letters or a decimal point as text.

q5.title: Order of execution when conversion fails
q5.prompt: Put these steps in the order they happen when `int(text)` raises `ValueError` inside a `try/except` block.
q5.hint: Arrange the code pieces in the order Python should run them.

help.concept: The pieces should form valid Python code in the order it should run.
help.hint_1: Start with the line that sets up the Python action.
help.hint_2: Place lines that depend on earlier values after those values exist.

tokens.t3: The rest of the `try` block is skipped

q7.prompt: Complete the code keyword that handles the error after `try`.
q7.hint: Read the sentence around the blank and decide what role the missing word plays.

q8.title: Choose the risky conversion
q8.prompt: Fill in the expression that can raise `ValueError` when the text is not a valid integer.
q8.hint: Use the built-in conversion from text to integer.

help.hint_1: The missing code should attempt to turn `user_text` into a whole number.
help.hint_2: Choose the expression that calls Python's integer conversion function on the variable.

q9.title: Catch a bad integer input
q9.prompt:
A number scanner might receive invalid text. Convert the input inside a `try` block, then print either the formatted number or an invalid message.

For example, if the input is `27`, your program should print:
`Number: 27`.

`Sum: 13`.

q11.title: Count valid numbers
q11.prompt:
A comma-separated list may contain bad entries. Loop through each part, try to convert it to `int`, count only the valid integers, and print the final count.

For example, if the input is `3,7,bad,10`, your program should print:
`Valid count: 3`.
q11.hint: Read the code or question carefully and choose the option that creates the exact behavior the prompt asks for.

s1.title: What try/except does
s1.bodyMarkdown:
`try` lets your program attempt code that might fail. If an expected error happens, `except` runs instead of letting the whole program crash.

```python
text = "42"

try:
    number = int(text)
    print(number + 1)
except ValueError:
    print("That was not a whole number.")
```

Step by step:
- `int(text)` tries to turn the string into an integer.
- If `text` looks like a number, the `try` block keeps going.
- If `text` cannot be converted, Python jumps to the `except ValueError` block.

For example, if `text` is `"42"`, the output is `43`.
If `text` is `"apple"`, the output is `That was not a whole number.`

s2.title: Only the risky line needs protection
s2.bodyMarkdown:
A common beginner pattern is to put the risky conversion inside `try`, then handle the problem in `except`.

```python
user_text = "9a"

try:
    score = int(user_text)
    print("Valid score:", score)
except ValueError:
    print("Invalid score")
```

Line by line:
- `user_text` stores text.
- `int(user_text)` is the risky step because some text cannot become an integer.
- If conversion works, `print("Valid score:", score)` runs.
- If conversion fails, Python skips that print and runs the `except` block.

This is useful when cleaning messy input, such as numbers typed by a user or values read from text data.

s3.title: Use exceptions to keep programs usable
s3.bodyMarkdown:
Without `try/except`, one bad value can stop the whole program. With it, you can show a helpful message and continue.

```python
first = "10"
second = "oops"

for item in [first, second]:
    try:
        value = int(item)
        print("Clean value:", value)
    except ValueError:
        print("Skipped bad value:", item)
```

What happens:
- The loop checks each text value.
- `"10"` converts successfully, so it prints `Clean value: 10`.
- `"oops"` raises `ValueError`, so it prints `Skipped bad value: oops`.
- The program keeps going instead of stopping on the bad value.
